summary:
The bill’s primary purpose is to ensure that Department of Health and Human Services grant program materials addressing opioid misuse and use disorders do not refer only to naloxone when discussing opioid overdose reversal drugs.

The bill directs the Secretary of Health and Human Services, when issuing regulations or guidance for any HHS grant program addressing opioid misuse and use disorders, to ensure that any reference to an opioid overdose reversal drug, including references to naloxone, is inclusive of any opioid overdose reversal drug approved under section 505 of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act for the emergency treatment of a known or suspected opioid overdose.

For existing HHS regulations or guidance issued before enactment, the bill requires the Secretary to update covered references within one year after enactment. These updates must make references to opioid overdose reversal drugs inclusive of any such drug that has been approved or otherwise authorized for use by the Food and Drug Administration.

The requirement for updating existing references applies to HHS regulations or guidance in two specified grant programs: the State and Tribal response to opioid use disorders grant program under section 1003 of the 21st Century Cures Act, commonly known as State Opioid Response Grants and Tribal Opioid Response Grants; and the grant program for priority substance use disorder prevention needs of regional and national significance under section 516 of the Public Health Service Act. The bill may be cited as the “Halting the Epidemic of Addiction and Loss Act of 2025.”
